title: Built an AI stock screener in using Financial Modeling Prep data and Agentic AI.
author: AffectionateFrame359
selftext:
I’ve been messing around with a side project since August 2025 and thought I’d share it here since I haven’t posted in a while.

  
Basically I built an AI-assisted stock screener using gpt and gemini apis and the Financial Modeling Prep API. It’s not anything super fancy with maths but kind of more like an automated research workflow that helps surface stocks and give some context on saving some time.

  
I started it with a £10k paper trading account on Trading 212 just to see if the ideas it was flagging actually made sense in practice.

  
peaked around £13.5k in Nov 2025  
dropped to \~£10.8k in March  
currently sitting around £12.0k

  
So nothing crazy, but it’s been interesting to see how the signals behave over time. Also kind of funny how this little side project has actually done better than the S&P this year so does anyone think the future of investing lies with AI and will become how large companies do their allocations?

replies.author: AffectionateFrame359
replies.body: I have the system self hosted on my computer via n8n if you have ever used it?

replies.body: I have not, how can I use it?

replies.author: vastlysabledumps
replies.body: n8n's solid for self-hosting but data latency can bite you on fast movers, how often you running the screener?

replies.author: AffectionateFrame359
replies.body: just once a week so far.

comments.body: I made one on an HHM model this weekend.  Backtesting it for a few weeks before I use any real money. Would love to chat about parameters

replies.body: Nice, how are you running your backtests it is something I haven't done yet.

comments.author: Either_Door_5500
comments.body:
Building a screener with LLMs is a great way to handle qualitative context, but relying on standard fundamentals APIs can get messy quickly. A major trap with standard commercial vendors is that they normalize data blindly, which often breaks down when an LLM tries to audit why a specific financial metric was flagged.

If you want to make your screener more robust, make sure you are capturing the raw XBRL facts alongside the normalized data. LLMs perform much better when they can see the exact context of how a company reported a line item in its 10-K versus how a vendor transformed it.

Happy to help if useful. I work on an api I built that provides SEC data for devs, so this comes up a lot for me.
